hint_star_mass_input: Star mass relative to the Sun. Determines everything: luminosity, temperature, lifetime, habitable zone. The Sun = 1. Red dwarfs 0.08–0.5, orange dwarfs 0.5–0.8, solar-type 0.8–1.2, hot stars 1.5+. Stars below 0.08 M☉ are brown dwarfs; above ~150 M☉ they are unstable.
hint_star_b_mass: Mass of the companion star. In binary systems, the secondary is usually less massive than the primary. Affects the combined luminosity and orbital dynamics of the pair.
hint_separation: Average distance between the two stars in the binary pair. Wider separation (>50 AU) makes S-type planetary orbits more stable. Close binaries (<1 AU) favor P-type (circumbinary) orbits.
hint_bin_eccentricity: Eccentricity of the binary stars' mutual orbit. High eccentricity reduces the stable zone for planets. Typical range: 0.0–0.8. Most binaries have eccentricity 0.3–0.6.
hint_luminosity: Total energy output relative to the Sun. Derived from mass via the mass-luminosity relation (L ∝ M^3.5 for main sequence). The Sun = 1. Determines habitable zone distance and planet temperatures.
hint_temperature: Surface temperature relative to the Sun (5778 K). Determines spectral class: O (>30000 K), B, A, F, G (Sun), K, M (<3700 K). Hotter stars are blue-white; cooler stars are red.
hint_radius_solar: Stellar radius relative to the Sun (696,000 km). Computed from luminosity and temperature: R = √(L) / T². Main-sequence stars range from 0.1 R☉ (red dwarfs) to 10+ R☉ (blue giants).
hint_lifetime: Time the star spends on the main sequence, relative to the Sun (~10 billion years). Massive stars burn out in millions of years; red dwarfs last trillions. Life needs at least 0.5–1 billion years to emerge.
hint_peak_wavelength: Wavelength of maximum emission (Wien's law: λ_max = 2,897,800 / T). The Sun peaks at ~502 nm (green-yellow). Determines what light reaches the planet and which photosynthetic pigments evolve.
hint_hz: Range of orbital distances where liquid water could exist on a rocky planet's surface. Inner edge: runaway greenhouse. Outer edge: CO₂ condensation. Depends on stellar luminosity.
hint_frost_line: Distance beyond which volatile compounds (water, ammonia, methane) condense into ice grains. Critical for planet formation: gas giants typically form beyond this line. ~2.7 AU for the Sun.
hint_sys_inner: Closest stable orbit around the star. Planets closer than this would be tidally disrupted or engulfed. Roughly 0.1–0.2 AU for solar-mass stars.
hint_sys_outer: Farthest stable orbit where the star's gravity dominates over galactic tidal forces. Defines the outer edge of the planetary system. Hundreds to thousands of AU for Sun-like stars.
hint_optimal_star: Stars between 0.6–1.4 M☉ are considered best for habitability. Lighter stars are too dim and tidally lock planets; heavier stars burn out too quickly for complex life to evolve.
hint_predicted_pigment: Predicted dominant color of photosynthetic organisms based on the star's spectrum (Kiang et al. 2007). Plants evolve pigments to absorb the most abundant wavelengths, reflecting what they don't need.
hint_absorbed_range: Wavelength range that flora would absorb for photosynthesis. Depends on the star's peak emission. On Earth, chlorophyll absorbs red (600–700 nm) and blue (400–500 nm).
hint_reflected_color: The color that plants would appear to an observer — the wavelengths they reflect rather than absorb. Earth flora reflects green because the Sun peaks near green-yellow.
hint_bin_period: Time for the two stars to complete one mutual orbit. Computed from separation and total mass via Kepler's third law. Ranges from days (close binaries) to millennia (wide pairs).
hint_combined_lum: Total luminosity of both stars together. Used to compute the combined habitable zone. Simply L_A + L_B for well-separated binaries.
hint_combined_hz: Habitable zone computed from the total luminosity of both stars. For S-type orbits, the planet mainly orbits one star, so the single-star HZ may be more relevant.
hint_s_type_max: Maximum stable orbital radius for a planet around one star in a binary system (Holman & Wiegert 1999). Planets beyond this distance are destabilized by the companion star's gravity.
hint_p_type_min: Minimum stable orbital radius for a circumbinary planet (Holman & Wiegert 1999). The planet must orbit far enough from both stars for its orbit to be stable.
hint_mass: Planet mass relative to Earth (5.97 × 10²⁴ kg). Determines gravity, atmosphere retention, and internal structure. Earth = 1, Mars ≈ 0.107, Neptune ≈ 17.1, Jupiter ≈ 317.8. Above ~10 M⊕ a planet starts accumulating thick gas envelopes.
hint_semi_major: Average distance from the host star — the semi-major axis of the orbital ellipse. Earth–Sun = 1 AU ≈ 149.6 million km. Determines the amount of starlight received, orbital period, and temperature.
hint_eccentricity: How elongated the orbit is. 0 = perfect circle, 1 = parabolic escape. Earth ≈ 0.017, Mars ≈ 0.093, Pluto ≈ 0.25. High eccentricity causes extreme seasonal temperature swings between perihelion and aphelion.
hint_axial_tilt: Angle between the planet's rotation axis and its orbital plane. Determines seasonal contrast. Earth = 23.4° (moderate seasons), Uranus ≈ 98° (extreme seasons), Venus ≈ 177° (retrograde, minimal seasons). Values 0–80° are considered habitable.
hint_star_mass: Mass of the host star relative to the Sun. Determines stellar luminosity, habitable zone location, and orbital period. The Sun = 1. Used together with semi-major axis to compute orbital mechanics.
hint_albedo: Fraction of incoming starlight reflected back to space (Bond albedo). Ranges 0–1. Earth ≈ 0.306 (clouds, ice, oceans), Venus ≈ 0.77 (thick clouds), Moon ≈ 0.12 (dark rock), Mars ≈ 0.25. Higher albedo → cooler planet.
hint_co2: Volume fraction of CO₂ in the atmosphere. Earth ≈ 0.0004 (420 ppm), Venus ≈ 0.965 (96.5%), Mars ≈ 0.953. CO₂ is the main greenhouse gas after water vapor. Even small increases cause significant warming.
hint_atmo_mass: Total atmosphere mass relative to Earth's (5.15 × 10¹⁸ kg). Controls surface pressure directly. Earth = 1, Mars ≈ 0.006 (thin, ~6 mbar), Venus ≈ 93 (crushing 93 bar), Titan ≈ 1.5.
hint_gravity: Surface gravitational acceleration relative to Earth (9.81 m/s²). Computed as g = M / R². Determines walking comfort, atmosphere retention, erosion rates, and maximum organism size. Mars ≈ 0.38, Jupiter ≈ 2.53.
hint_density: Average density relative to Earth (5.51 g/cm³). Indicates composition: <1 = mostly gas/ice, 1 = rocky like Earth, >1 = iron-rich core. Mars ≈ 0.71, Jupiter ≈ 0.24, Mercury ≈ 0.98.
hint_escape_velocity: Minimum speed to leave the planet's gravity, relative to Earth (11.19 km/s). Determines which atmospheric gases the planet can retain. Mars ≈ 0.45 (lost most atmosphere), Jupiter ≈ 5.32.
hint_surface_area: Total surface area relative to Earth (5.1 × 10⁸ km²). Proportional to R². Affects total habitable land area, ocean coverage, and biodiversity potential.
hint_volume: Total volume relative to Earth (1.08 × 10¹² km³). Proportional to R³. Related to internal heat budget and geological activity.
hint_aphelion: Farthest point from the star in the orbit. Computed as a × (1 + e). The planet receives least starlight at aphelion. Earth's aphelion ≈ 1.017 AU (July).
hint_perihelion: Closest point to the star in the orbit. Computed as a × (1 − e). The planet receives most starlight at perihelion. Earth's perihelion ≈ 0.983 AU (January).
hint_orbital_period: Time to complete one orbit (one year). Computed via Kepler's third law: P² = a³ / M_star. Earth = 1 year = 365.25 days. Mars ≈ 1.88 years, Jupiter ≈ 11.86 years.
hint_orbital_velocity: Average orbital speed relative to Earth (29.78 km/s). Faster orbits = closer to star or more massive star. Mercury ≈ 1.59, Mars ≈ 0.81.
hint_tropic: Latitude where the star reaches zenith at solstice. Equal to the axial tilt angle. Defines the tropics — the zone receiving direct overhead sunlight during the year. Earth: 23.4° (Tropic of Cancer/Capricorn).
hint_polar_circle: Latitude above which 24-hour daylight (or darkness) occurs at solstice. Equal to 90° − axial tilt. Earth: 66.6° (Arctic/Antarctic Circle). Smaller tilt = polar circles closer to poles = milder seasons.
hint_eq_temp: Temperature the planet would have with no atmosphere, based only on starlight and albedo. Earth ≈ 255 K (−18°C). The difference from actual temperature reveals the greenhouse effect's strength.
hint_greenhouse: Additional warming above equilibrium temperature from atmospheric greenhouse gases (mainly CO₂ and H₂O). Earth ≈ +33 K, Venus ≈ +510 K (runaway greenhouse), Mars ≈ +5 K.
hint_surface_temp: Estimated average surface temperature = equilibrium temp + greenhouse warming. Earth ≈ 288 K (15°C). Liquid water exists between ~273–373 K (0–100°C) at 1 atm pressure.
hint_surface_pressure: Atmospheric pressure at the surface, in Earth atmospheres. Computed from gravity and atmosphere mass. Earth = 1 atm (1013 mbar). Below 0.006 atm, liquid water cannot exist. Above ~10 atm, extreme conditions.
hint_scale_height: Altitude at which atmospheric pressure drops by a factor of e (≈2.718). Earth ≈ 8,500 m. Lower gravity → taller atmosphere. Affects cloud height, aircraft ceiling, and atmospheric escape rate.
hint_gas_retention: Which gases the atmosphere can retain, based on the Jeans escape criterion. A planet retains a gas if escape velocity > 6× the gas's thermal velocity at the exosphere. Lighter gases (H₂, He) escape most easily.
hint_in_hz: Whether the planet's orbit falls within the habitable zone — the range of distances from the star where liquid water could exist on a rocky surface. Based on stellar luminosity.
hint_habitable_tilt: Axial tilt between 0° and 80° is considered habitable. Extreme tilt (>80°) causes catastrophic temperature swings: polar regions bake while equator freezes, then reverses. Uranus-like tilt is hostile to complex life.
hint_planet_mass_moon: Mass of the parent planet relative to Earth. Determines the Hill sphere (gravitational reach for moons), orbital periods of moons, and tidal effects. Earth = 1, Jupiter ≈ 317.8.
hint_planet_radius_moon: Radius of the parent planet relative to Earth (6,371 km). Used to express moon distances in planet radii and compute angular sizes. Earth = 1, Jupiter ≈ 11.2.
hint_planet_density_moon: Average density of the parent planet relative to Earth (5.51 g/cm³). Used in the Roche limit calculation — denser planets tear apart moons at closer distances.
hint_planet_semi_major_moon: Distance from the planet to its host star. Determines the Hill sphere radius — the region where the planet's gravity dominates and moons can exist stably.
hint_star_mass_moon: Mass of the star the planet orbits. Together with orbital distance, determines how far the planet's gravitational influence extends (Hill sphere).
hint_moon_radius: Moon's radius relative to Earth (6,371 km). Our Moon ≈ 0.273 R⊕ (1,737 km), Ganymede ≈ 0.413, Titan ≈ 0.404. Determines surface area, gravity, and angular size in the sky.
hint_moon_density: Moon's average density relative to Earth (5.51 g/cm³). Our Moon ≈ 0.606, Io ≈ 0.644, Europa ≈ 0.547. Rocky moons have higher density; icy moons are lighter. Affects mass and Roche limit.
hint_moon_distance: Distance from the planet's center, in planet radii. Our Moon ≈ 60.3 R⊕. Must be above the Roche limit (moon is torn apart) and below the stable orbit limit (~1/3 Hill sphere).
hint_hill_sphere: Maximum gravitational reach of the planet — the region where its gravity dominates over the star. Moons must orbit well within this boundary. Earth's Hill sphere ≈ 0.01 AU ≈ 235 R⊕.
hint_stable_orbit: Practical limit for stable moon orbits — roughly 1/3 to 1/2 of the Hill sphere. Moons beyond this are gradually stripped away by stellar perturbations.
hint_moon_mass: Moon's mass computed from radius and density: M = ρ × R³. Expressed in Earth masses. Our Moon ≈ 0.0123 M⊕, Ganymede ≈ 0.025, Titan ≈ 0.0225.
hint_moon_gravity: Surface gravity of the moon relative to Earth. Our Moon ≈ 0.166 g. Determines whether the moon can hold an atmosphere and affects potential habitability.
hint_angular_size: Apparent size of the moon as seen from the planet's surface. Our Moon ≈ 31' (arcminutes). Determines eclipses (≈ Sun's apparent size → total eclipses) and tidal effects.
hint_moon_period: Time for the moon to complete one orbit around the planet. Our Moon ≈ 27.3 days. Computed via Kepler's third law from orbital distance and planet mass. Determines tidal rhythms.
hint_roche_limit: Minimum safe distance from the planet, in planet radii. Below this, tidal forces tear the moon apart (forming rings). Depends on density ratio between planet and moon. ~2.5 Rp for equal densities.
hint_orbit_valid: Checks that the moon's orbit is physically possible: it must be above the Roche limit (not torn apart) and below the stable orbit limit (not stripped by the star). Both conditions must be met.
hint_multi_moon: Checks orbital spacing between moon pairs. Moons with orbital distance ratio <1.3 are too close and will gravitationally scatter each other. Also detects near-resonances (2:1, 3:2, etc.) which can amplify perturbations.
flora_reasoning_ob: Star peaks in extreme UV. Surface flora is unlikely without a substantial ozone layer. Underwater photosynthesis may use scattered visible light.
flora_reasoning_a: A-type star peaks in UV/blue. Flora would absorb the abundant blue and green photons, reflecting longer wavelengths. Plants would appear red or deep orange.
flora_reasoning_f: F-type star peaks in blue. Flora absorbs the dominant blue and green wavelengths, reflecting yellow-orange. Plants would appear orange or golden.
flora_reasoning_g: G-type star (like our Sun) peaks near green-yellow. Flora absorbs red and blue photons (most energy-efficient) while reflecting green. This is why Earth plants are green.
flora_reasoning_k: K-type star peaks in orange-red. Flora absorbs the dominant orange/red photons and most visible light, reflecting the least useful blue wavelengths. Plants would appear blue-purple.
flora_reasoning_m: M-dwarf star is dim and peaks in infrared. Flora must absorb every available photon across all visible wavelengths to gather enough energy. Plants would appear black or very dark purple.
